svg.text: 1. Sort or order the input by the right key
2. Iterate; at each step, take the locally best choice
3. Never reconsider — past choices are final
Two properties must hold for greedy to be correct:
• Greedy-choice property: a locally optimal choice leads to a globally optimal solution
• Optimal substructure: the optimal solution contains optimal solutions to subproblems
When greedy works (and when it does not)
• Works: interval scheduling, Huffman coding, Dijkstra, Kruskal/Prim, fractional knapsack
• Fails: 0/1 knapsack, longest path, coin change with arbitrary denominations
• If greedy fails, fall back to DP — same problem shape, different solution method
• The greedy-choice proof is usually exchange argument or induction on choices
A wrong greedy can pass small tests and fail on large adversarial inputs.
Always prove the greedy-choice property before committing to greedy.
Greedy is fast when it works — but proving it works is the hard part.
